metadata.title: Build a Brand Voice Consistency Auditor with Claude
metadata.purpose: Show students in Nina Harris' Branding and AI course how to build a voice consistency pipeline that scores five copy samples on archetype alignment, flags drifting phrases, and rewrites the lowest-scoring sample in the correct register.
metadata.audience: Students and practitioners in a university branding course with an AI component
sources: Branding and AI, Chapter 10 + Chapter 5 (Nina Harris)

beats.narration_text: Merhaba — this is Liam, in for Bear. For Nina Harris' course, Branding and AI, here is the question we are running today: a committed archetype makes every copy decision a filter — does this fit? — instead of an open debate. But you cannot apply the filter to copy that has already shipped without checking it first. We are building the auditor that makes the drift visible before it ships or right after it does.

props.command: Brand voice audit. Archetype: Innocent. Voice adjectives: warmth, simplicity, honesty. Score these five samples on alignment (1–5), flag the drifting phrase, and rewrite the lowest-scoring sample in the correct Innocent register.
output: Sample 3: score 1 — 'Only 3 left' flags urgency, not warmth
Sample 5: score 2 — 'Last chance' is scarcity drift
Rewrite: 'The summer bundle is still here, whenever you're ready.'

beats.narration_text: Here is what breaks without the auditor. You have a committed Innocent archetype. You brief a copywriter on the brand voice. Three weeks later, your email subject line reads: only three left — grab yours before it is gone. Your growth team wrote urgency because urgency converts. The archetype says warmth because warmth holds. Without a per-sample check against the archetype, both decisions feel reasonable and one of them is wrong.

props.sparkLine: Both decisions felt reasonable. One was wrong.

beats.narration_text: The theory from Chapters Five and Ten. The archetype as forcing function: once chosen, every copy decision becomes a filter — does this fit the archetype's core motivation? The Innocent archetype's motivation is goodness and simplicity. Its shadow is denial and naivety. The voice that fits it: warmth, honesty, openness, no manufactured scarcity. Urgency language — only three left, last chance — imports the Magician or Ruler archetype's urgency into a brand that cannot carry it. The auditor makes the constraint cheap to enforce at the sentence level.

The centerpiece: five copy samples scored by Claude against the Innocent archetype and the three voice adjectives — warmth, simplicity, honesty. Sample one, five: fully consistent. Samples two and four, score four: minor variation, no flag. Sample five, score two: last chance is urgency drift, flagged. Sample three, score one, highlighted in terracotta: only three left — scarcity language that the Innocent archetype cannot carry. The rewrite at the bottom: the summer bundle is still here, whenever you're ready — same offer, warmth replaces urgency, same informational content. Source: Branding and AI, Nina Harris, Chapters Five and Ten.

Honesty about what the auditor does not do. Brand voice exists on a spectrum and deliberate tonal variation is sometimes intentional — a brand can shift register for humor or urgency in contexts where it genuinely fits. The auditor surfaces unintentional drift, not all variation. A one in five is not always wrong — sometimes the brand is experimenting. The auditor names the departure so the decision is deliberate rather than accidental.

artifactLines: Deliberate tonal variation is sometimes correct — the auditor flags it, not bans it.
A score of 1 is not always wrong — it may be an intentional register shift.
The auditor surfaces unintentional drift so the decision to keep it becomes deliberate.
The alignment score is a diagnostic, not an absolute truth about every piece.

beats.narration_text: Let us recap with Claude. Brand Voice Consistency Auditor. Four lines.

artifactLines: The archetype as forcing function: every copy decision becomes binary — fits or doesn't.
Five samples, one alignment score per sample, one flagged phrase per drift.
The lowest-scoring sample gets a rewrite in the correct register.
The auditor makes the constraint cheap to enforce at the sentence level.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Take this prompt into your Claude session: You are a brand voice auditor. My archetype is blank, and my three voice adjectives are blank, blank, blank. Here are five copy samples from my recent marketing: sample one through five — list them. For each sample score archetype alignment one to five, flag the specific word or phrase that drifts most from the archetype's expected register, and give a one-sentence rationale citing the archetype's core motivation or shadow. Then rewrite the lowest-scoring sample in the correct register, keeping the same informational content. Use your actual archetype — from the classifier in the previous video or your own judgment — and five real pieces of copy you have recently shipped. Look at where the flags land. They are showing you where urgency, scarcity, or a different archetype's vocabulary has crept in.

props.command: Brand voice audit. Archetype: [ARCHETYPE]. Voice adjectives: [ADJ1, ADJ2, ADJ3]. Score these five samples (1–5 alignment), flag the drifting phrase in each, give a rationale. Rewrite the lowest-scoring sample in the correct register: [SAMPLE 1–5].

beats.narration_text: Build a Brand Voice Consistency Auditor with Claude. Liam, in for Bear.

props.title: Build a Brand Voice Consistency Auditor with Claude
